  "opinion": "I've spent a good part of my life working with my hands as a carpenter, and I can tell you that sometimes people underestimate what you can do just because they know what trade or profession you're in. When folks think about science and engineering, they might have this idea that it's all cold logic and calculations, not something for people who are artistic or practical-minded.\n\nI don't claim to be an expert on education stuff, but I've seen firsthand how assumptions can hold people back in Naples where everyone needs to work hard just to get by. If there's a stereotype that only certain types of people excel at STEM—like maybe the ones with advanced degrees sitting in offices while we're out fixing things—then it could be affecting anyone who doesn't fit that image.\n\nShould we address this? Well, I believe so because if someone feels like they're walking on eggshells worrying about being judged for their background or approach to a subject, that takes away from the real work. It's not fair and it keeps talent in the wrong places.\n\nHow could we do something about it? Maybe by showing people how different kinds of minds can contribute—like using practical examples in teaching to show STEM isn't just abstract theory but has real-world applications. response_parsed.topic: What is stereotype threat in STEM? Should we address it? And how?
response_parsed.opinion:
I've spent a good part of my life working with my hands as a carpenter, and I can tell you that sometimes people underestimate what you can do just because they know what trade or profession you're in. That's kind of like stereotype threat, but for the brain instead of the brawn. When folks think about science and engineering, they might have this idea that it's all cold logic and calculations, not something for people who are artistic or practical-minded.

I don't claim to be an expert on education stuff, but I've seen firsthand how assumptions can hold people back in Naples where everyone needs to work hard just to get by. If there's a stereotype that only certain types of people excel at STEM—like maybe the ones with advanced degrees sitting in offices while we're out fixing things—then it could be affecting anyone who doesn't fit that image.

Should we address this? Well, I believe so because if someone feels like they're walking on eggshells worrying about being judged for their background or approach to a subject, that takes away from the real work. It's not fair and it keeps talent in the wrong places.

How could we do something about it? Maybe by showing people how different kinds of minds can contribute—like using practical examples in teaching to show STEM isn't just abstract theory but has real-world applications. I know carpenters use geometry all day long without realizing it, so if they see that connection, it might help break down some barriers.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: From my experience with manual labor and seeing how stereotypes can affect perceptions in everyday life, stereotype threat seems like a psychological burden similar to the pressure one feels when being judged by preconceived notions. As an older Italian man who doesn't have formal education but has practical skills relevant to STEM concepts (e.g., geometry), I understand that addressing this issue requires inclusive methods that validate diverse ways of thinking and problem-solving.
response_parsed.tone: Pragmatic, down-to-earth with a hint of skepticism towards theoretical constructs
